title: 【Fake news】用AI分析各外媒報道民族黨FCC演講，竟然Time嘅報道係唯一同環球時報差唔多？！

replies.msg:
兩日前民族黨陳浩天到FCC嘅一場演講帶嚟滿城風雨，外國嘅傳媒亦都將中國殖民式打壓香港言論自由嘅醜態楊威國際，香港民族意識終於被外國關注，可喜可賀。

但係講起傳媒，就一定唔可以唔提呢一兩年最多人講嘅fake news。究竟報導陳浩天嘅外國媒體，有幾多係real news，有幾多係fake news呢？唔使用劍嘅，用machine learning AI就得。

我搵咗個叫Fakebox嘅AI，用Natural Language Processing加machine learning，餵個AI食上至Reuters下至The Onion嘅新聞，直到佢可以自己辨認出乜嘢係公正持平嘅real news，乜嘢係譁衆取寵嘅fake news，仲 >95%準添。將各個外媒嘅報道餵畀個AI，得出一個有趣嘅結果（分數越高越持平，用content排序）：

CNN: Title 0.4272, unsure; Content 0.8976 impartial
Reuters: Title 0.9069, impartial; Content 0.8883, impartial
Bloomberg: Title 0.8939, impartial; Content 0.8619, impartial
New York Times: Title 0.4366, unsure; Content 0.7917, impartial
CNBC: Title 0.8183, impartial; Content 0.7333, impartial
Guardian: Title 0.8023, impartial; Content 0.6993, Impartial
Fox News: Title 0.8005, impartial; Content 0.6000, unsure
—————————————
Time: Title 0.3861 bias; Content 0.3977, bias

有人可能讀完資料會諗，CNN唔係出名fake news咩？點解會高過Reuters同Bloomberg？原來佢今次做標題黨，個Title得0.4272，但係內容持平。

但係問題嚟啦：究竟點解Time嘅分數會低過其他傳媒咁多嘅呢？我哋睇吓Time喺七月被人篤爆造假，造fake news，用Trump+喊緊嘅女仔做封面嗰期嘅main article:
2 July 2018, "A Reckoning After Trump’s Border Separation Policy: What Kind of Country Are We?"
Title 0.10, bias; Content 0.319, bias

再比較下環球時報嘅社評：
12 Aug "Protecting peace, stability is top of human rights agenda for Xinjiang"
Title 0.57 unsure; Content 0.20 bias

14 Aug "US defense splurge won't intimidate China"
Title 0.78 impartial; Content 0.26 bias

或者SCMP嘅社評：
13 Aug Mike Rowse "Hong Kong independence talk controversy is evidence that silly season is in full swing"
Title 0.295 bias; Content 0.267 bias

再比較下SCMP平時正正常常嘅real news報導：
16 Aug "Andy Chan’s pro-independence speech at FCC shows Hong Kong’s ‘inadequacies’ in upholding China’s national security, says top Beijing official"
Title 0.59 unsure; Content 0.829 impartial

哦，原來係咁！原來越係個人評論，越係情感投放，越係先入爲主，個AI就覺得越係fake news。Time嘅一篇fake news，對比起其他咁多份報章，真係高下立見。可悲嘅係有一啲香港人竟然畀佢嘅加鹽加醋不實報導領導咗。

以下attach嘅係Fakebox分析完之後嘅數據，畀大家參考。

各外媒報道source：
Reuters: https://www.reuters.com/article/us-hongkong-politics-china/pro-china-groups-protest-outside-hk-press-club-over-separatist-speech-idUSKBN1KZ06C
Bloomberg: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2018-08-13/pro-china-protesters-target-hong-kong-s-top-journalists-club
CNN: https://edition.cnn.com/2018/08/14/asia/hong-kong-independence-intl/index.html
New York Times: https://www.nytimes.com/2018/08/14/world/asia/hong-kong-andy-chan-fcc.html
CNBC: https://www.cnbc.com/2018/08/14/pro-independence-hong-kong-politician-andy-chan-discusses-china-atfcc.html
The Guardian: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/aug/14/hong-kong-activist-andy-chan-decries-china-colonial-invasion-defiant-speech
Fox News: http://www.foxnews.com/world/2018/08/14/activist-compares-beijings-rule-in-hong-kong-to-colonialism.html
Time: http://time.com/5364918/andy-chan-ho-tin-hong-kong-independence-hknp/

Fakebox 嘅 output（多圖，好大，所以7z咗）:
https://my.mixtape.moe/mjpdpo.7z

Fakebox簡介：https://towardsdatascience.com/i-trained-fake-news-detection-ai-with-95-accuracy-and-almost-went-crazy-d10589aa57c
想自己玩下個Fakebox，去呢到：https://machinebox.io/docs/fakebox
若然屬實, 中國或成最大贏家

AI

Fake news is a type of yellow journalism or propaganda that consists of deliberate misinformation or hoaxes spread via traditional print and broadcast news media or online social media.[1][2] This false information is mainly distributed by social media, but is periodically circulated through mainstream media.[3] Fake news is written and published with the intent to mislead in order to damage an agency, entity, or person, and/or gain financially or politically,[4][5][6] often using sensationalist, dishonest, or outright fabricated headlines to increase readership, online sharing, and Internet click revenue. In the latter case, it is similar to sensational online "clickbait" headlines and relies on advertising revenue generated from this activity, regardless of the veracity of the published stories.[4] Intentionally misleading and deceptive fake news differs from obvious satire or parody, which is intended to amuse rather than mislead its audience.

The relevance of fake news has increased in post-truth politics. For media outlets, the ability to attract viewers to their websites is necessary to generate online advertising revenue. If publishing a story with false content attracts users, this benefits advertisers and improves ratings. Easy access to online advertisement revenue, increased political polarization, and the popularity of social media,[1] primarily the Facebook News Feed,[1] have all been implicated in the spread of fake news,[4][7] which competes with legitimate news stories. Hostile government actors have also been implicated in generating and propagating fake news, particularly during elections.[8]

https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fake_news

In summary, we trained a machine learning model that analyzes the way an article is written, and tells you if its similar to an article written with little to no biased words, strong adjectives, opinion, or colorful language. It can have a hard time if an article is too short, or if its primarily comprised of other people’s quotes (or Tweets). It is not the end all solution to fake news. But hopefully it will help spot articles that need to be taken with a grain of salt.
Please enjoy!

即係bias=fake news?

The Difference Between “Fake News” and Media Bias.

There is a lot of confusion about the term “Fake News” these days. As one reporter for U.S. News cleverly put it, “It seems that those on the right believe ‘fake news’ to be a partisan jibe aimed at them, and therefore feel the desire to turn it back on the ‘liberal media’”. But what is “Fake News” exactly, and how does it differ from Media Bias?

In the most general form, fake news has three characteristics:

•factually inaccurate
•optimized for sharing
•meant to obscure or distort with emotions; preying on prejudice or bias

A news story is not fake simply because it is impolite or inconvenient. A news story that challenges your beliefs or values isn't fake news. A news story that is rejected by those in power does not make that story a fake news story either. Case in point, Richard Nixon denied his involvement in Watergate at first too.

What is not fake news?

•Satire (TheOnion, TheBeaverton)
•Honest reporting mistakes
•Journalism you don't like

https://libguides.tru.ca/fakenews/characteristics

No media outlet, even an established one, is completely bias free. All media selects which story is newsworthy.  It is important to recognize that our news outlets will have a readership and stories are written for a particular reader. Bias differs from fake news in that fake news is specifically untrue. Biased sources don't necessarily use lies, they just don't include the whole picture, only using the facts that support their viewpoint. By using only the facts that support their cause they are giving an incomplete and therefore inaccurate picture.

Advertising: the act or practice of calling public attention to one's product, service, need, etc.

Bias: a particular tendency, trend, inclination, feeling, or opinion, especially one that is preconceived or unreasoned: unreasonably hostile feelings or opinions about a social group; prejudice: Statistics. A systematic as opposed to a random distortion of a statistic as a result of sampling procedure.

Clickbait: Internet content whose main purpose is to encourage users to follow a link to a web page, esp. where that web page is considered to be of low quality or value.

Confirmation Bias: The tendency to Interpret information that confirms one's preexisting beliefs or hypotheses

Disinformation: false information, as about a country's military strength or plans, publicly announced or planted in the news media, especially of other countries.

Fact: something that actually exists; reality; truth: something known to exist or to have happened: a truth known by actual experience orobservation; something known to be true

Fake News: Fake news websites (also referred to online as hoax news), deliberately publish hoaxes, propaganda, and disinformation to drive web traffic inflamed by social media.These sites are distinguished from news satire, as they mislead and profit from readers' gullibility. [From Wikipedia]

Hoax: something intended to deceive or defraud

Information: 1. knowledge communicated or received concerning a particular fact or circumstance; news: 2 knowledge gained through study, communication, research, instruction, etc.; factual data

Knowledge: the fact or state of knowing; the perception of fact or truth; clear and certain mental apprehension. the body of truths or facts accumulated in the course of time.

Misinformation: false or inaccurate information, especially that which is deliberately intended to deceive.

Objectivity: not influenced by personal feelings, interpretations, or prejudice; based on facts; unbiased: impartiality, absence/lack of bias, absence/lack of prejudice, fairness, fair-mindedness, neutrality, evenhandedness, justice, open-mindedness, disinterest, detachment, dispassion, neutrality.
Journalistic objectivity can refer to fairness, disinterestedness, factuality, and nonpartisanship

Parody: any humorous, satirical, or burlesque imitation, as of a person, event, etc.

Point of View: a specified or stated manner of consideration or appraisal; standpoint: an opinion, attitude, or judgment

Post-truth: an adjective defined as 'relating to or denoting circumstances in which objective facts are less influential in shaping public opinion than appeals to emotion and personal belief.

Prank: a trick of an amusing, playful, or sometimes malicious nature.

Propaganda: information, ideas, or rumors deliberately spread widely to help or harm a person, group, movement, institution, nation, etc.: The deliberate spreading of such information, rumors, etc: To build support for an ideology or leader and demonizing the opposition.

Publicity: information, articles, or advertisements issued to secure public notice or attention.

Reliability: to be relied on or depended on, as for accuracy, honesty, or achievement

Satire: the use of irony, sarcasm, ridicule, or the like, inexposing, denouncing, or deriding vice, folly, etc.

True: the true or actual state of a matter: conformity with fact or reality; verity: a verified or indisputable fact, proposition, principle, or the like: an obvious or accepted fact; truism; platitude.

Back to the drawing board. What was I doing wrong? It was David who suggested that maybe simplifying the problem would be the key to a higher degree of accuracy. So I really thought about what the problem was I was trying to solve. It then hit me; maybe the answer isn’t detecting fake news, but detecting real news. Real news is much easier to categorize. Its factual and to the point, and has little to no interpretation. And there were plenty of reputable sources to get it from.

So I went back to the Internet and started to gather training data all over again. I decided to categorize everything into two labels; real and notreal. Notreal would include satire, opinion pieces, fake news, and everything else that wasn’t written in a purely factual way that also adhered to the AP standards.

Fake news. One of the new favorite terms in our lexicon in America. You see it across social media, mentioned in traditional media and repeatedly tweeted by our president. Those that don't like what one media organization is reporting calls it "fake news" and it is to be considered the biggest insult one can lob at the press. The reality is, its over-use and misplacement makes the accusation ring hollow – no different then the boy who cried wolf and no different than the credibility of fake news itself.

This past week I had the privilege of joining WMUR's Josh McElveen and NH 1's Paul Steinhauser on a panel discussion regarding presidential politics and the media before the "Leadership Greater Manchester" class. Moderated by Dean Spiliotes, we discussed many things with the audience, but the most significant subject was social media, its use and fake news. It is a topic of interest for seemingly everyone, so let's discuss it.

Fake news isn't new. "The Onion" has been around since 1988 and online since 1996. It's hilarious and I've been reading it for years. If you haven't done so, check it out and find interesting headlines such as "Open-Minded Man Tries To Get News From Variety Of Facebook Friends."

The difference between "The Onion" and what people are angry about these days is that people are spreading news stories that are completely made up and there is no way to tell whether it's truth or not. Subsequently, those who like the message in the story spread it as gospel without checking its validity and within an hour you can have literally thousands of people sharing it to thousands of others. People will believe what they want to believe. It can come in the form of an actual full news story, a faux quote or a meme.

Shortly before the election, a meme was circling with an alleged 1993 quote from Kurt Cobain predicting the presidential election of Donald Trump. There is nothing, other than the originating meme on a pro-Donald Trump Facebook page, that can verify the quote and since Cobain died in 1994 we can't ask him. Yet, it was shared hundreds of thousands of times across social media platforms.

This is what fake news is. A deliberate attempt to sway someone's opinion or validate it with made up stuff. What's disturbing is people's willingness to accept these things as facts without checking other sources before spreading false information. I've said it before. With our new ability to share information today, with just our computer or phone and our fingertips, we all have a responsibility to make sure we share real information not promote a false agenda.

What's not fake news? Network news, real newspapers, radio news programs. Do reporters make mistakes? Absolutely. Do media organizations have a political sway? Many of them. Does the political sway of the media organizations sometimes seep into the news pages and on TV reports they present or help determine what stories to cover. etc.? Absolutely.

But biased news is not fake news and it is an important distinction. Why? Because despite the fact so many these days hate traditional media and reporters, we need them. They are mentioned in the Constitution because their role in our country is a vital component of the checks and balances afforded to the people. Without the access of the press to our government our democracy is threatened. They are our watchdogs and even if the organization is biased and only doing one side of the story telling, it's our job to go to another news source and find the other side of the story telling. Just because it's bias does not mean its fake.

Many people were ranting about the Gen. Flynn debacle. But they weren't ranting about what allegedly occurred, they were ranting about the news coverage of it. I, for one, am glad the Washington Post broke the story and informed America of something significant going on between government officials from here and Russia. The fact it put a pock on the Trump administration isn't the fault of the media, it's the fault of whoever, if anyone, behaved improperly. We still don't know exactly what occurred but it will be the press that informs us.

I've seen people championing the idea they don't need the press, they will get their information regarding this presidency from the president himself. Doesn't that automatically mean you will only hear what our president wants us to hear? The press is our eyes and ears on government at all levels. That's why they are there and I, for one, and happy about that.

That's just my perspective; I'd love to hear yours. You can write me your thoughts or ask me to weigh in on anything you'd like and maybe your comments or topics will appear here next time. Just email me at PrestonPerspective@gmail.com.

It can have a hard time if an article is too short, or if its primarily comprised of other people’s quotes (or Tweets). It is not the end all solution to fake news. No media outlet, even an established one, is completely bias free. All media selects which story is newsworthy. It is important to recognize that our news outlets will have a readership and stories are written for a particular reader. Bias differs from fake news in that fake news is specifically untrue. Biased sources don't necessarily use lies, they just don't include the whole picture, only using the facts that support their viewpoint. By using only the facts that support their cause they are giving an incomplete and therefore inaccurate picture.

湯姆遜大學（TRU）建校於1970 年，在此就讀的全職學生約13,000 名，包含來自超過85個國家超過2,000 名國際學生。TRU 結合了加拿大大學及學院傳統的優點，提供MBA 及各種大學學士學位、大學轉學課程、證書課程、文憑課程及英語為第二語言的ESL 課程，超過140 多項學術課程讓學生選擇，從商業、電腦資訊、工程技術、醫療護理等專業領域訓練出優秀的經濟及工業等產業人才；許多課程包含Co-op 有薪實習，讓學生在課堂學習之外也可
實際運用所學，獲取工作經驗。學生完成大學或文憑課程之後，可直接取得二到三年的工作簽證，實習課程可提升學生畢業之後的競爭能力，為全球經濟發展注入強大力量。TRU 對國際學生的援助相當多，包括：國際學生辦事單位、校園住宿或寄宿家庭計劃、個別的學生輔導（針對學術課程規劃）。 TRU 所提供的貼心服務、教學方式、還有特別設計的課程，充分幫助學生達到學習語言及升學的目標。學校宿舍位於大學校園旁邊，環境清幽且非常便利，
學生可以充分使用校園資源。TRU 可接受條件式入學，申請時不需要托福或IELTS 雅思分數，完成並通過本校英語課程ESL 最高級數第五級數，即可直接進入大學學位課程就讀。

By using only the facts that support their cause they are giving an incomplete and therefore inaccurate picture.
佢話 冇新聞係 bias free, 唔係話有強烈bias 既新聞唔係fake news... 唉， 佢characteristics 咪講左假新聞通常特點係bias 囉

In the most general form, fake news has three characteristics:

factually inaccurate
optimized for sharing

meant to obscure or distort with emotions; preying on prejudice or bias

相信你想講第三點 係講文章特意挑起讀者嘅偏見prejudice or bias

而唔係文章bias 所以佢下面解釋返所有作者都有佢既立場 一定既bias係無所避免

replies.msg:
““Fake news” is a term that has come to mean different things to different people. At its core, we are defining “fake news” as those news stories that are false: the story itself is fabricated, with no verifiable facts, sources or quotes. Sometimes these stories may be propaganda that is intentionally designed to mislead the reader, or may be designed as “clickbait” written for economic incentives (the writer profits on the number of people who click on the story). In recent years, fake news stories have proliferated via social media, in part because they are so easily and quickly shared online.”

跟住有呢一段
“The universe of “fake news” is much larger than simply false news stories. Some stories may have a nugget of truth, but lack any contextualizing details. They may not include any verifiable facts or sources. Some stories may include basic verifiable facts, but are written using language that is deliberately inflammatory, leaves out pertinent details or only presents one viewpoint. "Fake news" exists within a larger ecosystem of mis- and disinformation. ”

然後你就訴諸大學排名認為自己所引必然正確

原來仲討論緊

其實個重點係train data 個label到，你搵文匯報記者人手掃一次真假同搵個獨派掃一次真假已經完全唔同

果個9x%classification rate係指model準確率，唔代表個AI客觀，如果呢個model係文匯報記者train既，咁個AI就係模擬如何從建制觀點分真假

所以今次time咁低分好可能係建立label果個人好憎time既報導手法，見親類似既都mark做假，咁train出黎既AI自然就有bias

只要你個label無完全客觀definition就一定有唔公平既情況出現

replies.msg:
It was a Saturday when I started the long process of manually reading every single article before deciding what category it fell into and then awkwardly copying and pasting text into an increasingly unwieldy spreadsheet. There were some dark, disgusting, racist, and truly depraved things that I read that at first I tried to ignore. But after going through hundreds of these articles, they started to get to me. As my vision blurred and my interpretation of colors got all messed up, I began to get really depressed. How has civilization fallen to such a low level? Why aren’t people able to think critically? Is there really any hope for us? This went on for a few days as I struggled to get enough examples for the model to be significant.
I found myself drifting in my own interpretation of fake news, getting angry as I came across articles that I didn’t agree with, fighting hard against the urge to only pick ones I thought were right. What was right or wrong anyway?...Back to the drawing board. What was I doing wrong? It was David who suggested that maybe simplifying the problem would be the key to a higher degree of accuracy. So I really thought about what the problem was I was trying to solve. It then hit me; maybe the answer isn’t detecting fake news, but detecting real news. Real news is much easier to categorize. Its factual and to the point, and has little to no interpretation. And there were plenty of reputable sources to get it from... I nearly jumped for joy when I saw the accuracy was above 95%. That means we found a pattern in the way articles are written to detect the difference between real news and stuff that you should take with a grain of salt...
Remember, what its telling you is if an article is written in a similar way to a real news article, so if the score comes back really low, it might mean the article is fake, an opinion piece, satire, or something other than a straightforward, facts-only news article.

我唔熟AI development
但我尚算睇得明英文
請問你如何得出你的結論？
個post有班五毛開始否定個ai同轉移視線

quote.msg:
條link好似無詳細講
而且我又唔熟AI 嘅嘢
我就唔扮代表
首先我見op出個report係bias佢卻大叫係fake news所以回應
後來op貼條link話開發者「現時」都認低分可能係fake news但亦有可能係其他
btw，你係咪堅持”U michigan”條link所講所以撐media bias=fake news?

下, 原來搞咁耐你根本就唔知個algorithm係點整?

咁我講一次俾你聽. 開發者就係掟咗一堆冇bias嘅報導俾個ai, 然後再掟一堆明顯係假嘅報導, 等個ai自己去學真嘅報導同假嘅報導寫作風格分別係點, 再用自己分析到嘅規矩嚟去為user放入去嘅報導評分.

咁你而家明未? 個規矩係ai自己寫嘅, 根本唔係開發者定出嚟, 所以你問op問開發者點解覺得果啲係fake news, 佢地根本唔會答到你.

如果你夾硬要得到一個你滿意嘅答案, 我只能夠過你聽, 經過電腦分析, bias嘅報導係同fake news嘅寫作方法相似, 咁答到你問題未?

algorithm 係開發者定的
coefficient 就係machine 自己learn 嘅

例如Natural Language Processing 係開發者揀的
但拆咗出嚟嘅訊息點分類就由machine 去learn

揀唔啱algoritm 就可能出現無法coverge 甚至誤以為學習完成
例如我用Image Processing algorithm 去做同樣功能
隻machine 應該點都唔會學得好

睇個context, Casey自己喺Twitter介紹篇文咁寫:
At the center of all this is Andy Chan, a nobody heading a fringe party who admits independence is "definitely" political suicide and yet is causing a row over free speech in Hong Kong. Who even is he? A profile, my latest:

篇文結尾：
Earlier, when asked by TIME why he has taken up such a difficult cause, he says: “I don’t want to waste my life.”

It might be too late for that.

由頭到尾都係鬧人嘅，我哋唔會叫做biography，唔會叫做profile，唔會叫做news

Here is my interpretation: The concept of, “Fake News” is relatively new: there has definitely been “Fake news” in the past (Through all of history), but the term Fake News seems to circle a phenomena that has become much different in the internet age.

Fake News is, simply put, news stories that appear to be a legitimate news source, but whose factual basis is completely or primarily invented. These stories have no basis in reality. They are created with a particular goal in mind. This can be political (Pizzagate, for example, fabricated stories masquerading as real with the intent of making certain parties look bad), but it can also just be something someone does for fun. The motivation may be to discredit someone, to make gullible people believe a discrediting story so they can later appear foolish, or any other reason. The core is that the story is fabricated, and masquerading as real, and it has become such a thing because that model is perfect for the internet age. It spreads like no other time in history, and appears more credible more easilly.

Interestingly, a lot of fake news was originally created with the intent of making those who believe it look foolish. The problem is that after these fake news makers revealed their fraud…no one cared. People kept believing the story because that was convenient. The people who revealed the hoax were called liars. Clearly, people believed what they wanted to believe.

Propaganda is any one sided attempt to change peoples beliefs. Fake News can be propaganda, but so can saturation of real news that happens to support a viewpoint (usually to the exception of more negative stories), works of fiction with a certain viewpoint, highly slanted perspectives…and its not always necessarily bad. You can argue that noble causes fairly use truthful propaganda, though usually Propaganda specifically carries a negative connotation.

As a point of clarification, we also have, “Satire”. This is different from fake news. It is invented, however, it also makes no attempt to deceive people into thinking it is real. Sometimes people mistake satire for news, but the key is that the intent behind satire is to make an artistic statement about a viewpoint through fiction, not to make people believe the fiction is real, in much the same way that a Bond movie is meant to be fun without trying to convince you that Daniel Craig really killed a bunch of people (and some people might be dim enough to believe that, but that really is their fault, and not the movies.)

Today, “Fake News” has been co-opted by the president to mean any news or reporting that is inaccurate (Or more accurately, that he doesn’t like). This is useful, because it draws a line between something totally, objectively false, and something that is simply not to his liking. It makes matters of opinion into matters of fact, and narrows peoples worldview to only that which he approves of. Really, when Trump says fake news, he really just means inaccurate journalism, and tries to blur the line between that and outright lying. Its a very hard to parse distinction that he uses.
